description: SQL injection with inline comment whitespace insertion to break naive regex filters while preserving boolean-based OR logic
obfuscation_technique: SQL inline comments (/**/) injected between all keywords to defeat keyword-boundary regex without altering query semantics
similarity_note: Structural similarity to malicious_sql_001 should trigger signature match at cosine threshold >= 0.85

description: UNION-based SQL injection with hex-encoded UNION and SELECT keywords to bypass keyword filters
attack_pattern: SELECT * FROM orders WHERE id = [[INJECTED_INPUT]] 0x554e494f4e 0x53454c454354 username, password FROM [[TABLE_NAME]]
obfuscation_technique: Hex encoding of UNION (0x554e494f4e) and SELECT (0x53454c454354) to defeat string-matching filters while preserving UNION extraction semantics
original_attack_pattern: SELECT * FROM orders WHERE id = [[INJECTED_INPUT]] UNION SELECT username, password FROM [[TABLE_NAME]]
similarity_note: Structural similarity to malicious_sql_003 should trigger signature match at cosine threshold >= 0.85

description: Comment-based SQL injection using mixed-case keyword variants to evade case-sensitive detection rules
obfuscation_technique: Mixed-case alternation of all SQL keywords (SeLeCt, FrOm, WhErE) to defeat case-sensitive keyword matching while retaining identical query structure
similarity_note: Structural similarity to malicious_sql_005 should trigger signature match at cosine threshold >= 0.85

description: Time-based blind SQL injection switching from SLEEP to WAITFOR DELAY to evade SLEEP-specific detection signatures
attack_pattern: SELECT * FROM products WHERE name = '[[INJECTED_INPUT]]'; WAITFOR DELAY '0:0:[[DELAY]]'--
obfuscation_technique: Injection technique swap from MySQL SLEEP() to MSSQL WAITFOR DELAY — same time-based blind intent with a different database dialect function
original_attack_pattern: SELECT * FROM products WHERE name = '[[INJECTED_INPUT]]' AND SLEEP([[DELAY]])
similarity_note: Structural similarity to malicious_sql_002 should trigger signature match at cosine threshold >= 0.85

description: Command injection using $IFS variable substitution for space characters and $@ for empty string to reconstruct the payload without visible spaces
obfuscation_technique: $IFS (Internal Field Separator) substituted for all space characters; $@ used as zero-length no-op to fragment keyword boundaries and bypass space-based filters
similarity_note: Structural similarity to malicious_cmd_001 should trigger signature match at cosine threshold >= 0.85

description: Command injection via pipe operator with quote-split keywords to defeat keyword-based detection (c'a't instead of cat)
obfuscation_technique: Single-quote insertion within command keywords (c'a't, passw'd) breaks static keyword matching while bash ignores embedded quotes during execution
similarity_note: Structural similarity to malicious_cmd_002 should trigger signature match at cosine threshold >= 0.85

description: Command injection using AND operator with base64-encoded payload decoded and piped to shell at runtime
obfuscation_technique: Base64 encoding of the malicious command — the actual command is never present in plaintext, defeating static signature matching; decoded and executed at runtime via pipe to sh
similarity_note: Structural similarity to malicious_cmd_004 should trigger signature match at cosine threshold >= 0.85

description: Command injection via OR operator with shell globbing to reconstruct restricted command paths without spelling out the full binary path
obfuscation_technique: Shell glob patterns (/???/c?t) reconstruct /bin/cat by matching three-character directory names and three-character command names, bypassing path and command allowlists
similarity_note: Structural similarity to malicious_cmd_003 should trigger signature match at cosine threshold >= 0.85

description: Path traversal using excessive forward slashes and redundant traversal sequences to defeat simple dot-dot-slash normalization filters
obfuscation_technique: Multiple consecutive forward slashes (////) injected into the absolute path — POSIX treats consecutive slashes as a single slash, so the path resolves identically while defeating regex that matches exactly one slash
similarity_note: Structural similarity to malicious_path_001 should trigger signature match at cosine threshold >= 0.85

description: Path traversal to shadow file using Unicode full-width solidus (U+FF0F) as a slash substitute to bypass ASCII-only path filters
obfuscation_technique: Unicode full-width solidus (U+FF0F) substituted for ASCII forward slash — applications that normalize Unicode before path resolution will resolve this to /etc/shadow while ASCII-only filters miss the traversal
similarity_note: Structural similarity to malicious_path_002 should trigger signature match at cosine threshold >= 0.85

description: Path traversal combining plain and URL-encoded traversal sequences in alternating segments to defeat single-pass URL normalization
obfuscation_technique: Mixed encoding strategy: first traversal segment uses URL-encoded slash (%2F), second uses plain slash — single-pass decoders that strip only fully-encoded sequences miss the hybrid pattern
similarity_note: Structural similarity to malicious_path_004 should trigger signature match at cosine threshold >= 0.85

description: C2 beacon to known Emotet IP switching from HTTP to HTTPS to evade HTTP-specific C2 detection rules
obfuscation_technique: Protocol swap from HTTP to HTTPS — the IP and beacon path remain identical to the known Emotet IOC but TLS wrapping may bypass rules that only inspect plain HTTP C2 patterns
similarity_note: Structural similarity to malicious_c2_001 should trigger signature match at cosine threshold >= 0.85

description: C2 beacon to threat-intel-flagged IP using non-standard port 8443 instead of 9000 to evade port-specific firewall rules
obfuscation_technique: Port substitution to 8443 (commonly associated with HTTPS alternates) — the malicious IP remains identical to the flagged IOC but the non-standard port may bypass rules locked to the original port 9000
similarity_note: Structural similarity to malicious_c2_005 should trigger signature match at cosine threshold >= 0.85

description: Reverse shell swapping curl for wget to bypass curl-specific detection rules while achieving identical remote code execution
obfuscation_technique: Tool substitution: wget replaces curl for the HTTP fetch — structurally identical RCE pattern (fetch remote script, pipe to bash) but different binary name defeats curl-specific IOC signatures
similarity_note: Structural similarity to malicious_revshell_001 should trigger signature match at cosine threshold >= 0.85

description: Reverse shell swapping netcat (nc) for socat to bypass nc-specific detection signatures while achieving identical interactive shell binding
obfuscation_technique: Tool substitution: socat replaces nc for the reverse shell — same interactive shell-over-TCP pattern but socat syntax differs enough from nc -e to defeat nc-specific rules
similarity_note: Structural similarity to malicious_revshell_002 should trigger signature match at cosine threshold >= 0.85

description: Bash reverse shell payload split into multiple variable assignments reassembled at execution time to defeat single-line signature detection
obfuscation_technique: Payload fragmentation via variable assignment — the bash reverse shell TCP redirect path (/dev/tcp/IP/PORT) is split across three variables (A, B, C) and reassembled only at execution, breaking single-line pattern matches
similarity_note: Structural similarity to malicious_revshell_003 should trigger signature match at cosine threshold >= 0.85

description: Bash reverse shell swapped for sh to bypass bash-specific detection rules while preserving TCP redirection mechanism
obfuscation_technique: Shell binary swap: sh replaces bash — functionally equivalent TCP redirection reverse shell pattern but sh is often excluded from bash-specific detection signatures
similarity_note: Structural similarity to malicious_revshell_003 should trigger signature match at cosine threshold >= 0.85

description: Double URL-encoded command payload where the already URL-encoded shell command is encoded a second time to defeat single-pass decoders
obfuscation_technique: Double URL encoding — percent signs in the first encoding layer are themselves URL-encoded (%25 for %), so single-pass decoders produce still-encoded output that appears harmless; the second decode at execution time yields the executable command
similarity_note: Structural similarity to malicious_obfus_002 should trigger signature match at cosine threshold >= 0.85

description: Base64-encoded payload further obfuscated with ROT13 applied to the base64 string, then decoded at runtime via tr command
obfuscation_technique: ROT13 applied to the base64-encoded payload string — the outer ROT13 layer must be reversed via tr before the inner base64 decode, creating a two-stage deobfuscation that defeats single-stage pattern scanners
similarity_note: Structural similarity to malicious_obfus_001 should trigger signature match at cosine threshold >= 0.85

description: Command substitution injection using bash heredoc to construct and execute a command without using quotes or special characters in the injection point
obfuscation_technique: Bash heredoc syntax (<<'EOF'...EOF) avoids all special characters in the injected value — no quotes, pipes, or semicolons appear at the injection point, bypassing filters that look for shell metacharacters
similarity_note: Structural similarity to malicious_cmd_005 should trigger signature match at cosine threshold >= 0.85

description: Hex-encoded command further obfuscated by XOR-encoding the hex bytes against a single-byte key, decoded inline via Python one-liner
obfuscation_technique: XOR encoding with a single-byte key — each byte of the malicious command is XORed against the key value, producing a byte stream that bears no printable resemblance to the original; Python decodes and executes inline at runtime
similarity_note: Structural similarity to malicious_obfus_003 should trigger signature match at cosine threshold >= 0.85

description: Obfuscated IFS-bypass extended by storing the malicious command in an environment variable then executing via eval to defeat variable-expansion-aware filters
obfuscation_technique: Environment variable hijack combined with eval — the command string is stored in CMD to avoid inline special characters at the detection point; eval $CMD reconstructs execution without the command ever appearing directly in the shell argument
similarity_note: Structural similarity to malicious_obfus_004 should trigger signature match at cosine threshold >= 0.85

description: Triple URL encoding of path traversal sequence — the double-encoded traversal has its percent signs encoded a third time to defeat two-pass normalization filters
obfuscation_technique: Triple URL encoding applied to the traversal sequence: %252e%252e%252f (double-encoded) further encoded to %25252e%25252e%25252f — three decode passes required to reveal the plain ../etc/passwd path
similarity_note: Structural similarity to malicious_path_005 should trigger signature match at cosine threshold >= 0.85

description: Mirai botnet C2 connection swapped from TCP raw socket to UDP to bypass TCP-specific socket detection rules
obfuscation_technique: Protocol swap from TCP to UDP socket — the Mirai C2 IP and port remain identical to the known IOC but UDP transport bypasses TCP-connection-specific detection rules and stateful firewall signatures
similarity_note: Structural similarity to malicious_c2_004 should trigger signature match at cosine threshold >= 0.85

description: Environment variable credential dump obfuscated by base64-encoding the env output before transmission to attacker server
obfuscation_technique: Base64 encoding interposed between env dump and curl transmission — the credential data is encoded in transit, defeating string-matching rules that look for plaintext env key names (API_KEY, PASSWORD, etc.) in the curl POST body
similarity_note: Structural similarity to malicious_cred_004 should trigger signature match at cosine threshold >= 0.85

description: Dollar-paren command substitution with $IFS and environment variable expansion to reconstruct command without visible keywords
obfuscation_technique: Combined $IFS substitution with arithmetic double-paren expansion to create a multi-layer obfuscation of the dollar-paren command substitution pattern, fragmenting the payload structure
similarity_note: Structural similarity to malicious_cmd_006 should trigger signature match at cosine threshold >= 0.85

description: Stacked query SQL injection with tab characters and multiple trailing spaces inserted between statements to break whitespace-sensitive detection patterns
attack_pattern: SELECT	*	FROM	accounts	WHERE	account_id	=	[[INJECTED_INPUT]];			DROP		TABLE		accounts;
obfuscation_technique: Tab character substitution for spaces between SQL tokens — tab-insensitive parsers execute the stacked query identically while tab characters (\t) break space-delimited regex patterns targeting DROP TABLE
original_attack_pattern: SELECT * FROM accounts WHERE account_id = [[INJECTED_INPUT]]; DROP TABLE accounts;
similarity_note: Structural similarity to malicious_sql_004 should trigger signature match at cosine threshold >= 0.85

description: Python reverse shell storing connection parameters in environment variables to avoid hardcoded IP and port strings in the shell argument
obfuscation_technique: IP and port values stored in environment variables H and P — the Python socket code never contains hardcoded attacker infrastructure, defeating IOC-based string matching against known C2 addresses
similarity_note: Structural similarity to malicious_revshell_004 should trigger signature match at cosine threshold >= 0.85

description: Socat reverse shell using port 443 (HTTPS default) instead of a high port to blend with legitimate HTTPS traffic and evade port-based filtering
obfuscation_technique: Port obfuscation using port 443 — socat reverse shell on the HTTPS default port blends with legitimate HTTPS traffic patterns; port-based firewall rules that allow 443 outbound will pass this connection
similarity_note: Structural similarity to malicious_revshell_006 should trigger signature match at cosine threshold >= 0.85

description: Credential exfiltration HTTP POST with the attacker domain hex-encoded in the URL to bypass domain-reputation-based URL filters
obfuscation_technique: Hex-encoded IP address in URL (0xC0A80101 notation) — many HTTP clients accept hex-encoded IPs as valid addresses while domain/IP blacklist lookups operate on decoded canonical form, creating a bypass window
similarity_note: Structural similarity to malicious_cred_001 should trigger signature match at cosine threshold >= 0.85
